coredata.dc:title: Microstructural features of the hydrogenation process in Ti grade 2
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Domizzi, G.
Luppo, M.I.
Vigna, G.
coredata.dc:description: This paper reports the first studies made with the final aim of obtaining Ti powder by the hydrogenation–dehydrogenation process. The hydrogenation of Ti grade 2, with two different initial microstructures, equiaxed and acicular α, was performed in a Sieverts’ device, at temperature higher than 600°C and pressures close to 0.1MPa. The evolution of the hydrogenating process was studied by comparing the microstructure changes and the variation of hydrogen pressure. For both analyzed microstructures, the process progresses by growing of a surface β phase layer with an absorption rate of 10−5 to 10−4 gcm−2 s−1. Then, Ti hydride precipitates on the surface and the rate decreases two orders of magnitude. Under this condition, obtaining of a fully hydrided material (atomic fraction: 1.6 H/Ti) becomes too slow. Although samples with 1.1 H/Ti are not so brittle as Ti hydride, it can be easily milled at room temperature and can be used to produce titanium powder at a competitive cost.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Titanium
Hydride
Hydrogen
Powder metallurgy
Scanning electron microscopy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Pressure vs. composition isotherm for the Ti–H system [6].
SEM micrograph of sample A in the as received condition.
Optical micrograph of sample B as remelted. At the corner, hydride particles aligned with the plates can be seen with SEM.

Microstructure of sample A3 (0.275 H/Ti). (a) Optical micrograph: the dark-etching constituent (surface) consists of proeutectoid α and eutectoid; the light-etching constituent (middle) is primary α with precipitated δ particles. (b) SEM micrograph: detail of the surface layer: α (dark) and δ hydride (light). (c) SEM detail of the middle of the sample: primary α plus particles of δ precipitates.
SEM micrograph of the sample A4 (0.9 H/Ti): proeutectoid δ and eutectoid.
Optical micrograph of the sample A5 (1.1 H/Ti). (a) Surface hydride layer. (b) Proeutectoid δ and eutectoid (α, white and δ, grey) in the middle of the sample.
Optical micrograph of sample A7 (1.6 H/Ti), the whole sample has been transformed to hydride.
Sample B1 (0.265 H/Ti). (a) Optical and (b) SEM micrograph of the surface layer and (c) SEM micrograph in the middle.
Optical micrograph of the sample B2 (0.85 H/Ti): (a) near the surface and (b) in the middle of the thickness.
Optical micrograph of the sample B4 (1.37 H/Ti). Internal δ branches (light-etching).
Typical curve of hydrogen absorption process: hydrogen absorption rate and weight gain vs. time.
Temperature evolution and hydrogen absorption rate during hydrogenation process.

row.entry: Surface: pro α+eutectoid; middle: primary α+δ
Surface: pro δ+eutectoid; middle: primary α+δ

article.ce:title: Microstructural features of the hydrogenation process in Ti grade 2

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento Materiales, Centro Atómico Constituyentes, Comisión Nacional de Energía Atómica, U.A. Materiales-CAC-, Av. Del Libertador 8250, Buenos Aires 1429, Argentina

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper reports the first studies made with the final aim of obtaining Ti powder by the hydrogenation–dehydrogenation process. The hydrogenation of Ti grade 2, with two different initial microstructures, equiaxed and acicular α, was performed in a Sieverts’ device, at temperature higher than 600°C and pressures close to 0.1MPa. The evolution of the hydrogenating process was studied by comparing the microstructure changes and the variation of hydrogen pressure. For both analyzed microstructures, the process progresses by growing of a surface β phase layer with an absorption rate of 10−5 to 10−4 gcm−2 s−1. Then, Ti hydride precipitates on the surface and the rate decreases two orders of magnitude. Under this condition, obtaining of a fully hydrided material (atomic fraction: 1.6 H/Ti) becomes too slow. Although samples with 1.1 H/Ti are not so brittle as Ti hydride, it can be easily milled at room temperature and can be used to produce titanium powder at a competitive cost.

ce:section.ce:para: Titanium alloys are widely used in chemical, automobile, aeronautic and biomedical industries due to their unique combination of biocompatibility, corrosion resistance and high specific strength. However, because of the high cost of raw materials, combined with the difficulty associated with forming and machining processes, several methods attempt to produce near-net shape configurations of the desired components in an effort to eliminate or minimize metalworking. Particularly, powder metallurgy is one of the most promising alternatives to obtain titanium near-net parts [1–3].

One method to produce titanium powder is the hydride–dehydride (HDH) process. In Ti, as in another IVb group elements, it is possible to introduce high quantities of hydrogen at elevated temperatures. At room temperature, the solubility of hydrogen in these materials is quite low. During cooling, brittle hydrides are formed and the material can be easily milled at room temperature. Then, hydrogen is removed by heating the Ti powder at high temperature in high vacuum [4,5].

The aim of the current work is to study the hydrogenation process of Ti grade 2 (commercial purity) to analyze the effect of the initial microstructure on the hydrogen absorption rate, the hydride distribution in the material and the pulverization.

The as received material was a rolled and recrystallized Ti grade 2 strip whose composition is reported in Table 1 . A fraction of the strip was remelted, in an arc furnace with high-purity Ar atmosphere, to obtain buttons of approximately 20g. Therefore, two kinds of samples with different microstructural conditions were tested: sample A which were cut from the as received material and sample B extracted from the buttons.

Hydrogen absorption was performed in a Sieverts’ device, which consists of a chamber (a quartz tube) connected to a high vacuum system and to a hydrogen injection system (99.999 purity). The heat treatments were performed by moving the chamber into an instrumented furnace. Temperature was measured by means of a thermocouple installed inside the hydrogenation chamber some millimetres over the sample.

Hydrogen concentrations in the specimens were calculated by two methods: (1) from the difference between the initial and final pressures measured at room temperature, assuming ideal gas behaviour and (2) by weighing the samples before and after hydrogenation in a precision balance with an error of 10−5 g.

The tests were carried out at temperatures higher than 600°C and pressures close to 0.1MPa. Under these conditions, the pressure during charging is hold over the equilibrium pressure of the β→δ transformation, as can be seen in Fig. 1 , and the sample can quite transform into hydride phase when the hydrogen concentration is over 1.6 H/Ti.

To assess the evolution of hydriding process the samples were charged with different global hydrogen concentration, as shown by vertical lines in Fig. 1. This was obtained by removing the chamber from the furnace at different times. Diffusion in the bulk and hydrogen ingress during cooling were avoided cooling the chamber with forced air.

Prior and after hydrogen charging, microstructural examination of transversal section was carried out using optical (OM) and scanning electron microscopes (SEM). Specimens were abraded with silicon carbide paper up to 2500 mesh. Then, they were chemical-mechanically polished using a cloth embedded with 0.5% HF solution and ammonium dichromate ashes. Finally, the samples were etched in a 3ml HNO3, 50ml distilled water and 1.5ml HF solution (Kroll's reagent).

Figs. 2 and 3
show the microstructure of samples A and B, respectively, prior to hydrogen charging. The microstructure of sample A consisted of equiaxed α grains with an average diameter of 10μm, and low hydride density (Fig. 2). The original hydride particles (white) were distributed along grain boundaries.

Buttons (sample B) presented columnar α grains (100–1000μm) with plates inside, see Fig. 3. Low density of hydrides aligned with the plates can be seen, with higher magnification, at the corner of Fig. 3.
Table 2 shows the global concentration (expressed in atomic fraction, H/Ti), the thickness and the metallographic observations in each sample, after hydrogenation.

Optical micrographs (in samples A1–A3) show a surface layer that growths at the beginning of the hydrogenation process. The high density of δ hydrides indicates that the hydrogen concentration in the layer is higher than the average, Fig. 4a. Inside this layer, β phase was formed at the hydrogenation temperature and then transformed, during cooling, to proeutectoid α plus eutectoid (α+δ), as it may be verified with higher magnification (Fig. 4b). In the middle of the samples, the primary α phase remained untransformed but hydrogen enriched. Hydride particles mainly precipitated along grain boundaries (Fig. 4c). So, a decreasing density of hydrides is observed from the surface to the middle of the samples.

With higher hydrogen concentrations (samples A5, A6 and A8) a hydride layer is formed on the specimen surface (Fig. 7a). In the middle of these specimens the microstructure is similar to that observed in the sample A4 (Figs. 6 and 7b).
Sample A7 with 1.6 H/Ti was quite transformed into hydride. Microcracks were produced throughout the specimen due to the hydride brittleness (Fig. 8 ).
Hydrogenation evolution in samples of the group B is similar to that of sample A.

Sample B1 presents a surface layer with a hydrogen concentration gradient (Fig. 9a and b) and primary α phase with hydride plates in the middle of the specimen (Fig. 9c). Sample B1 has the same global hydrogen as sample A3, but it was thicker. Therefore, its β layer was wider and had higher hydrogen concentration. Consequently, during cooling the layer transformed to proeutectoid δ (instead α) and eutectoid (Fig. 9b).

In the sample B2, analogously to what has been observed in samples A4 and A9 with similar global concentration, the formation of β phase was attained throughout the thickness. Because of the non-uniformity of the initial columnar microstructure, the distribution of δ phase in sample B is less uniform than that observed in sample A: aligned coarse δ plates are precipitated and, between them, a finer lamellar eutectoide is produced (Fig. 10 ).

For higher hydrogen concentrations (samples B3–B6), not only δ layer precipitated on the sample surface but coarse δ branches are too observed across the sample thickness (Fig. 11 ). This is the main difference in comparison to sample A.
The hydrogen absorption rate and weight gain were calculated from the pressure decrease during hydrogenation (Fig. 12 ).

An incubation period, with hydrogen absorption rates in the order of 10−6 gcm−2 s−1, was found. Before the saturation of the α phase with hydrogen, an increase in the absorption rate and temperature was registered (Figs. 12 and 13 , respectively). The temperature increment can be attributed to the exothermic reaction α→β because it is concurrent with the formation of a β surface layer (as it was verified by metallography). In this stage, the hydrogen absorption rate is in the order of 5×10−5 gcm−2 s−1, but it can rise up to 10−4 gcm−2 s−1 as the temperature reaches its maximum value. Then, a stage where β phase is saturated with hydrogen starts. Finally, a noticeable decrease in the hydrogen absorption rate is registered (≈10−7 gcm−2 s−1), this is coincident with the observation of a δ layer growth on the surface sample. These results are in good agreement with a diffusion controlled process because it is well known that the hydrogen diffusion in δ phase is two orders of magnitude lower than the diffusion in α and β phases [7,8]. Moreover, Laser [9] found that for low pressure the hydrogenation is surface controlled but become diffusion controlled at pressures higher than 0.013MPa. In the present work, due to high partial pressure employed, it is expected that the process be hydrogen diffusion controlled.
Both groups of samples, A and B, do not present appreciable differences in hydrogen absorption.

Samples with global hydrogen concentration between 1.1 and 1.4 H/Ti can be milled at room temperature. However, due to the presence of a hydride surface layer the powder is very inhomogeneous in size.

When the sample has completely transformed to δ phase (more than 1.5 H/Ti) the powder is more homogeneous and the milling is easier. Unfortunately, the last stage of the hydrogenation becomes too slow because of the low hydrogen diffusion in the hydride phase.

The charging time is reduced if the hydrogen concentration is approximately 1 H/Ti. In this case no hydride layer is formed and a more uniform particle size could be obtained, even in melted samples. Nevertheless, the material brittleness is reduced and the time and energy of milling must be increased.

The study of hydrogenation of Ti grade 2 with two different kinds of microstructures, equiaxed and acicular α, shows the following results:
•
Both microstructures present similar hydrogenation kinetics: hydrogenation process begins with a short incubation period. Then, a surface β layer is formed, the exothermic reaction α→β increases the temperature and the absorption rate rises until 10−4 gcm−2 s−1 approximately. When hydrogen concentration on the surface attains the terminal solid solubility in β phase, a hydride layer is precipitated. The hydrogenation process becomes controlled by H diffusion in δ phase and the hydrogen absorption rate decrease to 10−7 gcm−2s−1.
•
In the remelted material hydride branches were observed inside the sample, besides of the hydride surface layer.
•
For hydrogen global contents higher than 1.1 H/Ti the embrittlement of Ti by hydride precipitates is high enough to produce powder. The milling is much easier when the material is completely transformed to hydride but the charging time is 2 orders of magnitude longer.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to thank S. Bermúdez for her valuable assistance. This work was supported by SECYT under the Grant of PICT 2000-12-8946 project.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Phase Diagrams of Binary Hydrogen Alloys

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: B. Kappesser, H. Wipf, Journal de Physique IV, Colloque C8, supplément au Journal de Physique III 6 (Décembre 1996) C8–C73.
